question: Design a `Playlist` class that contains a dynamically allocated array of `Song` objects. The `Song` class has `title` and `artist`. `Playlist` should manage adding and removing songs (demonstrating composition with dynamic memory).

test_cases.input:
add TitleA;ArtistA
add TitleB;ArtistB
print
remove TitleA;ArtistA
print
add TitleC;ArtistC
print
remove TitleX;ArtistY
print
remove TitleB;ArtistB
print
remove TitleC;ArtistC
print
test_cases.expected:
Playlist:
"TitleA" by ArtistA
"TitleB" by ArtistB
Playlist:
"TitleB" by ArtistB
Playlist:
"TitleB" by ArtistB
"TitleC" by ArtistC
Playlist:
"TitleB" by ArtistB
"TitleC" by ArtistC
Playlist:
"TitleC" by ArtistC
Playlist:
(empty)

test_cases.input:
add First;One
add Second;Two
add Third;Three
print
remove Second;Two
print
add Fourth;Four
print
test_cases.expected:
Playlist:
"First" by One
"Second" by Two
"Third" by Three
Playlist:
"First" by One
"Third" by Three
Playlist:
"First" by One
"Third" by Three
"Fourth" by Four

driver: std::string command, title, artist; Playlist myPlaylist; while (std::cin >> command) { if (command == "add") { std::cin.ignore(); std::getline(std::cin, title, ';'); std::getline(std::cin, artist); myPlaylist.addSong(Song(title, artist)); } else if (command == "remove") { std::cin.ignore(); std::getline(std::cin, title, ';'); std::getline(std::cin, artist); myPlaylist.removeSong(Song(title, artist)); } else if (command == "print") { myPlaylist.printPlaylist(); } else if (command == "copy_test") { Playlist copiedPlaylist = myPlaylist; std::cout << "Copied Playlist:\n"; copiedPlaylist.printPlaylist(); } }
errors: The `addSong` and `removeSong` methods are highly inefficient. They reallocate a new array of the exact required size for every add or remove operation, involving a new memory allocation and copying all existing elements. This leads to O(N) complexity for each operation, resulting in O(N^2) for N operations, rather than amortized O(1) for adds (with capacity doubling) and O(N) for removes (with shifting).
Variable names like `s_arr`, `cnt`, and `cap` are not descriptive, reducing code readability and making it harder to understand their purpose without comments or context.
